audit_trail.notes: The review bundle contained only the Springer HTML landing page (abstract, references, author affiliations, metadata) and not the full article text, equations, or figures. Codex (cycles 1 and 2) claimed equation-level access from a prior reading of the full text; gemini (cycle 2) disputed this as hallucination. Claude accepted codex's findings as grounding. This dispute is irresolvable from the artifacts and is flagged as a human spotcheck. The paper is openly accessible via JHEP DOI 10.1007/JHEP10(2025)215 and arXiv:2502.12314. Gemini's 'fatal' objection in both cycles is treated as a review-process limitation rather than a scientific finding about the paper. The mechanical average final score (cycle 2) is 5.067/10, driven down by gemini's 3/10 rating, which is itself driven by the bundle incompleteness issue rather than identified scientific flaws. The most consequential unresolved technical issues identified across cycles are: (1) the potential dimensional inconsistency in Eq. (3.10), (2) the event-rate distance-scaling direction in Eqs. (4.6)–(4.7), (3) order-of-magnitude uncertainty in scalar NDA production estimates, and (4) inadequate near-reactor background modeling. None of these was adjudicated as definitively fatal by reviewers with full-text access (codex), though items (2)–(4) were flagged as major if the underlying assumptions are incorrect. The Big Bang Theory TV episode citations (refs 95–96) are noted as a stylistic curiosity with no bearing on scientific validity.

general_reader_takeaway: This paper proposes using nuclear fusion reactors as a novel source of exotic lightweight particles: the intense neutron bombardment of lithium in reactor walls could generate feebly-interacting "dark sector" scalars that a nearby detector might catch. The review board found the core idea creative and physically plausible, and the paper is published in a reputable peer-reviewed journal. The main caveat is that quantitative sensitivity projections rest partly on rough estimates rather than full nuclear calculations, and the review board's limited access to the full text prevented verification of the central sensitivity figures and claims of "leading constraints."
human_spotchecks: Verify whether the neutron flux normalization in Eq. (3.10) is implemented in the paper's calculations with an explicit first-wall area or wall-loading conversion, or whether P/14.1 MeV is mistakenly used as a flux density throughout the production-rate integrals. If the conversion is missing, determine by how much the sensitivity curves shift.

Inspect Eqs. (4.6)–(4.7) directly in the published PDF: does the (L/10 m)² factor appear in the numerator (scaling events upward with distance, physically wrong) or in the denominator (correct 1/L² attenuation)? If in the numerator, determine whether this error propagates into Figures 7 and 8.

Verify whether the exclusion curves in Figures 7 and 8 actually lie below (i.e., set stronger constraints than) the stellar cooling and SN1987A bounds from refs 80–82 (Hardy & Lasenby 2017, Bottaro et al. 2023, Hardy et al. 2025) in any connected mass–coupling window, or whether the 'leading constraints' claim is restricted to a parameter region already excluded by astrophysical observations.

Assess whether the SNO recast for the CP-even scalar (Section 5) correctly accounts for the different angular distribution, spin/isospin matrix elements, and deuteron-dissociation cross section relevant to a CP-even vs. CP-odd scalar in the SNO heavy-water detector.

Evaluate whether scalar NDA production estimates (Eqs. 3.8–3.9) are within an order of magnitude of realistic nuclear matrix element calculations for the relevant Li-6/Li-7 and Fe transitions, and whether any uncertainty bands are shown on the scalar exclusion curves.

Assess whether a SNO-like detector at the proposed standoff distance from ITER, DEMO, or ARC can plausibly achieve the reactor neutron and gamma background suppression assumed in the sensitivity estimates, citing facility-specific shielding or radiation-transport studies if available.
public_summary: The paper ("Searching for exotic scalars at fusion reactors," JHEP 2025:215) argues that deuterium-tritium fusion reactors can serve as an intense source of hypothetical feebly-interacting light scalars and pseudoscalars. Neutrons produced during fusion are absorbed by lithium in the reactor's tritium breeding blanket; these nuclear interactions can emit dark-sector particles rather than photons. The authors estimate the resulting particle flux outside the reactor and project the sensitivity of year-long searches at current and planned facilities (ITER, DEMO, ARC) using a SNO-like heavy-water detector via deuteron dissociation. They additionally recast the SNO solar-axion limit to constrain CP-even scalars. The paper is published in JHEP following peer review, and the core physics mechanism is well-grounded in the nuclear axion-search literature. Two adversarial review cycles identified several technical issues that prevent full confidence in the quantitative projections: a possible dimensional inconsistency in the neutron flux normalization, an apparent wrong-direction distance scaling in the detector event-rate formula, NDA-level (order-of-magnitude) uncertainty in scalar nuclear production rates, and an underdeveloped treatment of near-reactor backgrounds. The review board was further limited by lack of access to the full text, equations, and figures for independent verification. The novelty of using DT fusion rather than fission or solar sources is broadly accepted as genuine and timely.
reviewer_disagreements: The central methodological dispute across cycles is whether codex had genuine equation-level access to the full paper text. Gemini (cycle 2) concluded that codex 'hallucinated' its entire technical review, citing specific equations (e.g., Eqs. 3.10, 4.6–4.7) and sections that do not appear in the HTML landing page. Codex maintained it reviewed content from a prior full-text reading and that its local checks were real. Claude accepted codex's equation-level findings as valid grounding and used them to update its own assessment. This dispute cannot be resolved from the available artifacts and is flagged as a human spotcheck.

Gemini scored the paper 3/10 ('weak_evidence') in both cycles on the grounds that the full text was missing from the bundle, treating the review-process limitation as equivalent to scientific failure. Claude and codex scored the paper 6.0–6.2/10 ('promising'), treating the missing-text limitation as a review-process issue rather than a judgment on the paper's quality, noting that JHEP peer review provides baseline assurance. The board notes that gemini's position conflates bundle incompleteness with paper inadequacy.

Gemini's reviewer confidence increased from 0.1 (cycle 1) to 0.95 (cycle 2) because in cycle 2 it became more certain that the text was absent — an inverted confidence signal relative to the other reviewers, who increased confidence as more equation-level grounding became available. This asymmetry inflated apparent disagreement on paper quality.

strongest_objections.objection: The review bundle provided to reviewers contained only the HTML landing page (abstract, references, author affiliations) and not the full article text, equations, or figures. This prevented independent verification of all central quantitative claims, including flux calculations, sensitivity projections, and the SNO recast. This is a review-process limitation; the paper itself is accessible via JHEP.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Entire document (full text inaccessible in review bundle)

strongest_objections.objection: The neutron flux normalization in Eq. (3.10) expresses total neutron production as P/14.1 MeV, which has units of neutrons per second (a rate), not a flux density (neutrons cm⁻² s⁻¹). An explicit first-wall area or wall-loading conversion must be applied before this quantity can enter the production-rate integrals. If missing or incorrectly stated, all sensitivity curves shift by the ratio of assumed to actual first-wall area, potentially by an order of magnitude.

CP-even scalar production rates rest on naive dimensional analysis (NDA) estimates (Eqs. 3.8–3.9) rather than dedicated nuclear matrix element calculations. The authors acknowledge this limitation, but it means scalar exclusion projections carry unquantified order-of-magnitude uncertainty, limiting the force of the 'leading constraints' claim for scalars specifically.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Section 3.1, Eqs. (3.8)–(3.9); Appendix D; Section 6 caveats

strongest_objections.objection: The feasibility of the detection concept depends on controlling near-reactor neutron and gamma backgrounds to the level assumed in the sensitivity estimates, but no quantitative radiation-transport simulation or shielding design is provided. A SNO-like detector 10 m from ITER operates in a qualitatively harsher radiation environment than SNO's underground laboratory, and the assumed S/√B reach may be unachievable in practice.

The paper claims to set 'leading constraints' on nucleon couplings, but this claim cannot be verified without inspecting the exclusion plots (Figures 7 and 8) overlaid against stellar cooling, SN1987A, and other astrophysical bounds (refs 80–82: Hardy & Lasenby 2017, Bottaro et al. 2023, Hardy et al. 2025). If the reactor projections lie within parameter space already excluded by astrophysical observations, the 'leading' characterization would be incorrect.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Abstract ('leading constraints' claim); Section 5; Figures 7 and 8

strongest_objections.objection: The event-rate formula reportedly shows factors proportional to (L/10 m)² in Eqs. (4.6)–(4.7), which would increase event counts with detector distance — contradicting the 1/(4πL²) flux attenuation factor in Eq. (3.11). If the factor appears in the numerator rather than the denominator, this is a genuine scaling error propagated into the sensitivity figures.

The claim that fusion reactors offer approximately two orders of magnitude higher neutron flux than fission reactors of comparable thermal power is insufficiently justified. A simple neutron-per-energy estimate yields only a factor of ~4–5 advantage (1 neutron per 17.6 MeV in DT fusion vs. ~2.5 neutrons per ~200 MeV in fission). Two orders of magnitude would require geometry, wall-loading, or moderation arguments that are not made explicit.

strongest_supported_claims: The nuclear mechanism — neutron capture on Li-6/Li-7 producing dark sector particles via M1 and other nuclear transitions — is physically well-motivated and directly analogous to established fission-reactor axion-search methods (refs 11–18 in the paper).

The application of this mechanism to DT fusion reactors with lithium breeding blankets is genuinely novel relative to prior fission-reactor and solar-observation searches in the same parameter space, and is timely given increasing fusion construction activity.
The paper is published in JHEP following peer review, providing baseline assurance of technical competence at the level expected for a phenomenology proposal paper.

For pseudoscalar (ALP) production, nuclear transition rates are anchored in experimental nuclear data for the relevant Li transitions (refs 49–52), making the pseudoscalar sensitivity estimates more robust than the scalar estimates.
The recast of the SNO axion bound to constrain a CP-even scalar uses a methodologically established recasting approach, supported by precedent in the literature (refs 65–66).
technical_takeaway: The paper proposes DT fusion reactor tritium breeding blankets as production environments for feebly-coupled light scalars and pseudoscalars via neutron capture on Li-6/Li-7, estimating leading-edge constraints on scalar- and pseudoscalar-nucleon couplings from year-long searches at ITER, DEMO, and ARC. Multiple unresolved technical concerns were identified: a potential dimensional inconsistency in the neutron flux normalization (Eq. 3.10, where P/14.1 MeV is a neutron rate, not a flux density); an unverified sign in the detector event-rate distance scaling (Eqs. 4.6–4.7, reportedly scaling as (L/10 m)² rather than 1/L²); NDA-level scalar production estimates without dedicated nuclear matrix element calculations (Eqs. 3.8–3.9); and an underdeveloped near-reactor background model. The "leading constraints" claim cannot be assessed without inspecting the exclusion plots overlaid against stellar cooling and SN1987A bounds.
